title: Notes
text: NOTES CHAPTER 1 1. Emily Dickinson, The Complete Poems of Emily Dickinson (Boston: Little, Brown, and Company, 1924). CHAPTER 5 1. Clarence Robinson, “Soviets Push for Beam Weapon,” Aviation Week & Space Technology , May 2, 1977, 17. Note that Aviation Week also used the unusual (though correct) term “gores” to describe the metal beams, just as Pat Price had done years earlier. CHAPTER 8 1. The website of Paranthropology , http://paranthropology.weebly.com . 2. Charles D. Laughlin, “Transpersonal Anthropology, Then and Now,” Paranthropology , February 6, 2010, http://paranthropology.weebly.com/charles-d-laughlin.html . 3. Jack Hunger, “Developing Trance,” Paranthropology , http://paranthropology.weebly.com/developing-trance.html . 4. Michael Grosso, “Nine Reasons To Fear the Paranormal,” The Anomalist , www.anomalist.com/commentaries/reasons.html . 5. George P. Hansen, The Trickster and the Paranormal (Philadelphia: XLibris, 2001), www.tricksterbook.com . 6. The villagers in this account referred to Tarbell as Cagliostro, as if he were the reincarnation of Cagliostro. “Il y a quelques années, un Cagliostro vint au château des Husson. C’était un étranger, personne ne l’avait vu avant, ni ne savait exactement d’où il venait et où il allait. Mais il fit des miracles. Un jour, Madame Husson avait des invités à dîner; elle dit qu’elle regrettait de ne pouvoir servir du sucre avec le café. Alors cet homme étrange, Cagliostro, se leva et trouva dans l’air, assez de sucre pour tous les invités. Ensuite il alla au Monastère et pris de pitié pour les Soeurs, les orphelines et les blessés, il leva les mains et produisit encore du sucre. Ensuite une centaine de livres de sucre arriva chez les Soeurs qui purent conserver tous les fruits. Le sucre était apparut dans l’air, Monsieur, matérialisé dans l’air. Oui, Monsieur, matérialisé dans l’air. Oui, Monsieur nous avons vu un miracle.” CHAPTER 9 1. The SPR is still going, see www.spr.ac.uk . 2. For reviews and meta-analyses of research areas such as mind-matter interaction, mind-organism interaction and dream ESP, see D. Radin, Entangled Minds: Extrasensory Experiences in a Quantum Reality (New York: Pocket Books, 2006). 3. D. W. Moore, “Three in Four Americans Believe in Paranormal: Little Change from Similar Results in 2001,” Gallup , June 16, 2005, www.gallup.com/poll/16915/three-four-americans-believe-paranormal.aspx . 4. Ralph Waldo Emerson, Lectures and Biographical Sketches , ed. E. W. Emerson (Boston: Houghton Mifflin, 1883), 177. 5. See Rupert Sheldrake’s website: www.sheldrake.org/Onlineexp/portal/mobiletelepathy.html . 7. “The Way In Is the Way Out: An Interview with Daniel Pinchbeck,” New World Disorder 3 (2005). 8. M. Eliade, Shamanism: Archaic Techniques of Ecstasy , trans. W. R Trask (Princeton, NJ: Princeton University Press, 1972). Originally published in French in 1951. 9. “In the Tsunami’s Wake,” Fortean Times , 196 (2005): 26. 10. “Did Animals Sense Danger?” Fortean Times , 194 (2005): 6. 11. M. Kittenis, P. G. Caryl, and P. An Investigation into the Ontology of Entheogenic Entity Encounters,” Entheogen Review: The Journal of Unauthorized Research on Visionary Plants and Drugs 17, no. 1 (2008): 1–9 and 38–40. CHAPTER 15 1. Stuart W. Twemlow, Glen O. Gabbard, and Fowler C. Jones, “The Out-of-Body Experience: A Phenomenological Typology Based on Questionnaire Responses,” American Journal of Psychiatry 139, no 4 (1982): 450–55. 2. Robert Waggoner, Lucid Dreaming: Gateway to the Inner Self (Needham, MA: Moment Point Press, 2009). 3. Thomas Campbell, My Big Toe (n.p.: Lightening Strike Books, 2003). 4. Gabbard, With Eyes of the Mind: An Empirical Analysis of Out-of-the-Body States (New York: Praeger, 1984). 8. Ian Wilson, interview by Robert Waggoner, The Lucid Dream Exchange , June 2010, www.dreaminglucid.com/dreamspeak.html . CHAPTER 16 1. J. A. Cheyne, S. D. Rueffer, and I. R. Newby-Clarke, “Hypnagogic and Hypnopompic Hallucinations during Sleep Paralysis: Neurological and Cultural Construction of the Nightmare,” Consciousness and Cognition 8 (1999): 319–37. 2. P. Marquet, J. Peters, J. Aerts, G. Delfiore, C. Deguelfre, A. Luxen, and G. Franck, “Functional Neuroanatomy of Human Rapid-eye-movement Sleep and Dreaming,” Nature 383 (1996): 163–66. 3. M. Winkelman, “Spirits as Human Nature and the Fundamental Structures of Consciousness,” in Shaman to Scientist , ed. J. Houran (Lanham, MD: Scarecrow Press, 2004), 59–96. 4. J. A. Hobson, Dreaming: An Introduction to the Science of Sleep (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2002). Hobson and other dream researchers still debate if REM = dreaming, but we can safely say that most dreams we remember come from this physiological state. 5. The best introduction to Jung is his autobiography, Memories, Dreams, and Reflections . James Hillman’s book The Soul’s Code (New York: Warner Books, 1996), while not explicitly about dreams, showcases his theory of the personality and its inner workings. 6. R. Bly, A Little Book on the Human Shadow (New York: HarperOne, 1988). 7. For more about myth, landscape, and unconscious acting out, see C. Chalquist, Terrapsychology: Reengaging the Soul of Place (New Orleans: Spring Journal Books, 2007). 8. S. Sherwood, “Relationship between the Hypnagogic/Hypnopompic States and Reports of Anomalous Experiences,” Journal of Parapsychology 66 (2002): 127–50. Stan Krippner and Montague Ullman’s work at the Dream Laboratory of the Maimonides Medical Center in the 1970s has proven to be the zenith of scientific work on psychic dreams; but the evidence is well known to lifelong dreamers. 9. H. Hunt, Multiplicity of Dreams (New Haven, CT: Yale University Press, 1989). 10. T. Takeuchi, Y. Miyasita, Y. Sasaki, M. Inugami, and K. Fukuda, “Isolated Sleep Paralysis Elicited by Sleep Interruption,” Sleep 15 (1992): 217–25. 11. D. Young and J. Goudet, Being Changed: The Anthropology of Extraordinary Experience (Ontario: Broadview Press, 1994). 12. J. Conesa-Sevilla, “Geomagnetic, Cross-cultural and Occupational Faces of Sleep Paralysis: An Ecological Perspective,” Sleep and Hypnosis 2 (2000): 105–11. 13. B. Tedlock, “The New Anthropology of Dreaming,” in Dreams: A Reader on the Religious, Cultural, and Psychological Dimensions of Dreaming , ed. K. Bulkeley (New York: Palgrave, 2001), 249–64. 14. P. Devereux, Haunted Land (London: Piatkus Limited, 2001). 15. See J. Campbell and B. Moyers, The Power of Myth (New York: Doubleday, 1988). 16. For a review of the scientific inquiry into psi, ESP, and dream telepathy, I recommend C. Tart, The End of Materialism: How Evidence of the Paranormal Is Bringing Science and Spirit Together (Oakland, CA: Harbinger Press, 2009). 17. D. Hufford, The Terror That Comes in the Night (Philadelphia: University of Pennsylvania Press, 1982). 18. E. Jones, On the Nightmare (New York: Liveright Publishing, 1951). Jones was a student of Freud’s, and interpreted many SP experiences as repressed sexual urges. 19. K. Briggs, Encyclopedia of Fairies (New York: Penguin Books, 1976). 20. B. Froud, Good Fairies, Bad Fairies (New York: Simon and Schuster, 1998). A whimsical guide, mixed with original folklore research and captivating illustrations. 21. B. Stoker, Dracula , as quoted in D. Hufford, The Terror That Comes in the Night (Philadelphia: University of Pennsylvania Press, 1982), 228. CHAPTER 21 1. R. H. Allen, Star Names: Their Lore and Meaning (Mineola, NY: Dover, 1963), 392–93. CHAPTER 23 1. See Stanislav Grof, M.D., “Ken Wilber’s Spectrum Psychology,” http://primal-page.com/grofken.htm . A wonderful book by Grof, which is really a must read for those interested in these subjects, is Grof’s recent offering, When the Impossible Happens: Adventures in Non-Ordinary Reality (Boulder, CO: Sounds True, 2005). Particularly interesting are Grof’s accounts of his meeting with Swami Muktananda, as well as his chapter on his experience with 5-MeO-DMT. 2. Here’s but one website dedicated to Ramana Maharishi (or just “Maharshi”): www.sriramanamaharshi.org . One book I would highly recommend that everyone read or re-read is Paramahansa Yogananda’s Autobiography of a Yogi (n.p.: Council on Self-Realization Fellowship, 1998), particularly the chapter entitled “The Resurrection of Sri Yukteswar,” as that goes into some detail about what the “subtle” (astral) and “causal” planes are like. Not that we should take this is as the last word on the subject, but just to familiarize ourselves with one highly influential view. 3. Grof quotes John Perry: “True mystics occasionally reactivate regressive complexes on their way to mature unity states,” and goes on to say: “In spite of the fact that Ken acknowledges frequent mysterious invasion of transpersonal insights in psychotic patients, mysticism remains for him miles apart from psychosis. It represents for him a purely transegoic progression, whereas psychosis is primarily characterized by a regression to early infancy in the service of the ego.” “Ken Wilber’s Spectrum Psychology,” http://primal-page.com/grofken.htm . 4. See Ken Wilber, The Eye of the Spirit: An Integral Vision for a World Gone Slightly Mad (Boston: Shambhala, 1998), 165–85. In these pages, Wilber for the first time in writing confronted Grof’s criticisms of his work. This needs to be read along with Grof’s essay in response (see note 1 ). 5. Excerpted from a 2001 interview between Wilber and Piers Clement. 6. There’s a discussion of what might have been the cause of Terence’s death at www.shroomery.org/forums/showflat.php/Number/1696452 . 7. Ganga White, Yoga Beyond Belief: Insights to Awaken and Deepen Your Practice (Berkeley, CA: North Atlantic Books, 2007). Danny Paradise is another top yoga teacher who has been an influential advocate of plant sacraments for many years. For an interview with him in which he directly speaks to some of the same issues, check out the following podcast on the website The C-Realm Radiant Sun: http://kmo.livejournal.com/408508.html . 8. From email correspondence with White and from speaking with one of his students, I understand that even though White made that statement, he still has been rather guarded about what he makes public about his use of plant sacraments. He apparently will only talk about it with his students when the subject is broached. I sense that this will all change in the next couple of years, that there will be far more openness in the yoga community about these subjects. 9. You can watch the video of their dialogue, “Asanas and Ayahuasca,” at www.realitysandwich.com/asanas_and_ayahuasca . You will also want to read all of the comments posted. My own view of what some called a “fiasco” is that it might have gone over better if Daniel had been dialoguing with a yogini, such as Padmani, who is both a yoga teacher and a regular user of plant sacraments like ayahuasca. 10. I also sense that it is precisely those of us who have been perhaps overly physically oriented in our life and yoga practice that actually need a high dose of something, anything to bust through the layers of egoity. This idea will take a good deal of unpacking and I’ll leave that for a separate piece. CHAPTER 25 1. Of course, these moments get complicated by more conceptual thoughts and feelings—for example, are we really hungry, or do we want to eat because we’re sad? But even without being complicated with thoughts, we’d still feel rooted in our bodies through hunger, thirst, energy, health, horniness, etc. 2. Albert Soesman, Our Twelve Senses (Stroud, UK: Hawthorn Press, 1998), 10–12. 3. Albert Soesman, Our Twelve Senses (Stroud, UK: Hawthorn Press, 1998), 16–17. 4. Whether you accept these bodies as “real” or not isn’t as important here as adopting the way they are organized to discuss other ideas. But there is plenty of scientific and spiritual evidence that this organizing principle is worthwhile. See the anthology Toward a Phenomenology of the Etheric World , by Jochen Bockemühl (Spring Valley, NY: Anthroposophic Press, 1985) and the work of Wolfgang Schad for some clear evidence-based examples. 5. A thoughtful and beautiful, though incomplete, examination of this concept appears in The Meaning of Love by Vladimir Solovyov (Hudson, NY: Lindisfarne Press, 1985). 6. Acts of intentionality are, according to Rudolf Steiner, acts of freedom. His picture of intention and freedom is complex and illuminating. This can be found in all his work, but it takes its most prominent place in the aptly entitled The Philosophy of Freedom (London: Rudolf Steiner Press, 2001). 7. Usually a T (for transgender) is added, making it GLBT. I’ve left it out here not to exclude, but so as not to equate all the freedoms, pressures, and social issues that come with being a transgendered person—especially when it comes to awareness of the body. So much more can be said about the deep understanding of the body that transgendered public figures (writers, performers, etc.) have brought to the world. For the purposes of this essay, I leave them out. I also leave the T out here because of a later story in this essay that recounts an encounter with my GLB college group. At the time, it was not truly conceptually inclusive of transgendered people. 8. The picture is, of course, more complex when historical factors (such as the invention of the terms homosexual and heterosexual) and cultural factors are considered. I’m writing mostly of the Western experience from the early twentieth century to the present day. 9. In the otherwise reasonable and progressive book, Empire of Illusion (Nation Books, 2009). 10. Again, members of the GLB community do this by coming to terms with the separations they feel between what their bodies’ wills and what society tells them. So it’s not surprising that many of those who have done pioneering work in mental health have also studied concepts gender and sexuality. The works of Jessica Benjamin are a good example. 11. A quick but perhaps grossly oversimplified example of what is meant here: a sign of health is being able to experience sadness about an event—say the loss of a pet—but being able to make a joke about it later without abandoning the grief . And to eventually feel “better” without losing the ability to reflect with love and sadness on the lost pet. One state does not disqualify the other, and one state is not better than the other, because a person can move amongst them. 12. David Loftus, Watching Sex: How Men Really Respond to Pornography (New York: De Capo, 2002). 13. I don’t imply here that all spiritual systems get it wrong, only that it becomes even easier to miss mistakes about the object-body in non-Abrahamic traditions, because many are considered to be comparatively “peaceful” or “progressive.” This, of course, is the same sort of misconception that leads people to think that science is purely objective. 14. A good and quick exploration of this is featured in Martha Stout’s excellent book, The Sociopath Next Door (New York: Three Rivers Press, 2006). 15. Of course, the object-body isn’t all good, nor is it evil. There are individuals who have distorted impulses in their object-bodies and respond erotically and sexually—for not entirely understood reasons—to violence. I don’t mean to abandon exceptions here, but I don’t wish to dwell on them, either. CHAPTER 26 1. C. Peter Fenwick and Elizabeth Fenwick, The Truth in the Light (London: Headline, 1997), 25–26. 2. Corliss Lamont, The Illusion of Immortality (New York: Continuum, 1990), 76. 3. Corliss Lamont, The Illusion of Immortality (New York: Continuum, 1990), 86–108. 4. Lamont’s portrayal of psychic research is extremely superficial, and contains several false and misleading statements. For an excellent critique of Lamont’s book, exposing a mass of inconsistencies and non sequiturs, see C. J. Ducasse, A Critical Examination of the Belief in a Life after Death (Springfield, IL: Thomas, 1961), chap. 13. 5. F. C. S. Schiller, Riddles of the Sphinx (London: Swan Sonnenschein, 1891), 295. 6. Cyril Burt, ESP and Psychology (London: Weidenfeld and Nicolson, 1975), 60.
